cases.title: Rollover MVC — Sequencing the Primary Survey
cases.presentation: You arrive at a single-vehicle rollover. A restrained 28-year-old male is still in the driver's seat, awake but confused, with noisy breathing and a bleeding scalp laceration that is soaking his collar. The scene is secured by fire.
cases.question: After scene size-up, what is the correct first action in the initial assessment?
options: Apply a traction splint to the legs
Control the actively bleeding scalp laceration, then assess LOC and airway
cases.explanation: Using CABC, life-threatening external bleeding is controlled first (direct pressure to the scalp), then you assess level of consciousness and immediately address the noisy airway. The generalized mechanism warrants a rapid trauma survey to follow.
cases.clinicalPearl: Noisy breathing is obstructed breathing—address airway sounds the moment you hear them rather than waiting to finish the survey.

cases.title: Deciding Load-and-Go
cases.presentation: A 45-year-old struck by a car is found supine. During the rapid trauma survey you note a tender, rigid abdomen and an unstable pelvis. He is pale and diaphoretic with a thready radial pulse.
cases.question: What is the most appropriate decision at this point?
options: Complete a full secondary survey on scene before moving
Declare load-and-go, package, and transport immediately with IV access en route
Stay on scene to start two large-bore IVs and infuse 2 liters
cases.explanation: A tender/rigid abdomen and unstable pelvis with signs of shock indicate internal hemorrhage—a critical trauma situation. This patient is load-and-go: transport immediately, begin IV fluid therapy en route, and notify the trauma center early.
cases.clinicalPearl: Unstable pelvis plus bilateral femur fractures or a rigid abdomen should make you anticipate and treat for hemorrhagic shock.

cases.title: Ongoing Exam Catches a Deteriorating Patient
cases.presentation: En route, your previously stable chest-trauma patient becomes increasingly anxious and short of breath. On your Ongoing Exam, breath sounds are now absent on the right and his neck veins appear more distended than before.
cases.question: What does the Ongoing Exam most likely reveal, and what is indicated?
options: Developing tension pneumothorax — prepare for needle decompression
cases.explanation: New absent breath sounds on one side with rising JVD, hypoxia, and hypotension indicate a developing tension pneumothorax. The Ongoing Exam exists precisely to catch this evolution so you can decompress promptly.
cases.clinicalPearl: Repeat the Ongoing Exam after every condition change—deterioration during transport is common and treatable when caught early.
